content:
Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation Xiaoheng Jin , Derrick Ng

, Xing Wu , Durga Acharya , Celesta Fong , Zongli Xie *

CSIRO Manufacturing, Private Bag 10, Clayton, VIC, 3168, Melbourne, Australia

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Hydrogen separationSilica membraneBTESERheology

A B S T R A C T Hydrogen-selective membranes are pivotal in advancing sustainable energy technologies. Organosilica, due to its hydrothermal stability, has shown promise as a gas separation membrane, but its fabrication requires stringent substrate pore control. This study investigates the influence of substrate pore size and rheological property on bis (triethoxysilyl)ethane (BTESE)-derived membranes. Through sol-gel synthesis modifications, including surfactant-induced gelation, pore filling was reduced while gas selectivity was enhanced on larger-pore sub­strates (20–50 nm). Rheological analyses confirmed that interparticle force modulation enables shear thickening behavior and results in defect-free membranes with superior hydrogen sieving. These findings expand the scope of BTESE-derived silica membranes for industrial gas separation, highlighting the role of substrate interaction and rheological control in membrane performance.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: 1. Introduction The global transition to clean energy and sustainability has placed hydrogen at the forefront of future energy solutions [1]. Hydrogen offers substantial environmental advantages over conventional fossil fuels due to its high energy density and produce only water as a byproduct upon combustion. Several technologies have been developed and imple­mented for hydrogen production and purification, such as pressure swing adsorption [2], and membrane separation [3]. Membrane sepa­ration has been successfully applied in gas separation and offers many advantage such as cost effectiveness, easy maintenance, simple con­struction and high separation performance [4,5]. Inorganic membranes such as dense metal alloys [6], porous ceramic [7], zeolite [8], silica [9] and carbon [10] have attracted widespread attention owing to their chemical/thermal resistance, excellent hydrogen separation perfor­mance and outstanding mechanical stability. Silica has been the most extensively studied membrane material due to its excellent gas sieving performance. However, the microporous silica membranes are unstable in harsh conditions due to the dissociation of siloxane groups (Si–O–Si) under hot and moist conditions [11], leading to defect formation as a result of segregated Si–OH groups. Recently, hybrid organosilica membranes have shown considerable promise [12]. These membranes have demonstrated hydrothermal sta­bility along with high hydrogen selectivity.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: silica membranes have shown considerable promise [12]. These membranes have demonstrated hydrothermal sta­bility along with high hydrogen selectivity. In place of the hydrothermally unstable siloxane groups the organic bridging groups (Si-R-Si) exhibited resistance to degradation in harsh environments, making hybrid silica membranes suitable particularly for industrial gas separation applications. Bis(triethoxysilyl)ethane (BTESE) is one of the most studied organosilica precursors used for the fabrication of orga­nosilica membranes via the sol-gel method [11,13]. It has offered excellent gas sieving properties, tenable properties and ease of fabrica­tion. However, scaling up BTESE-derived silica membranes remains challenging due to its low viscosity and strict requirement for substrate with 1–2 nm pores, which is often difficult to produce. Silica membrane is typically synthesized via a sol-gel method which includes solution preparation, coating, gelation and calcination. Scal­ability of BTESE-derived silica membrane was greatly limited by the coating and gelation. A common sol-gel material for ceramic membrane is Boehmite [14]. Boehmite sol usually forms a layer on substrates with pore size 2–5 times larger than its particle size [15]. The ability to stay on larger pores is due to the high viscosity of boehmite when concen­trated [16], and also the orthorhombic crystal structure with lamellar layers that prevent the infiltration of late coming particles [17].

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: when concen­trated [16], and also the orthorhombic crystal structure with lamellar layers that prevent the infiltration of late coming particles [17]. These properties are not found in BTESE sols. In hydrolysis of BTESE mono­mers, cyclization is desired over dimerization as it produces sub-nanometer sized rings which are responsible for the gas sieving properties of membrane [18]. Cyclization of BTESE monomers results in spherical nanoparticles [19] because of the low degree of crystallinity of

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: Available online 7 February 2025 0376-7388/Crown Copyright 2025 Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/bync/4.0/ ). obtained colloid particles. These optimal conditions for cyclization lead to low concentrations and small spherical colloids which meet strict substrate requirements mentioned above. Concentration of post-hydrolysis BTESE sol, however, triggers dimerization and makes the nanoparticles quickly incompatible with the solvent [20]. Due to these restrictions, it is challenging to produce BTESE solutions with suitable rheological properties that achieves effective coating on porous substrates. Recent advances in sol-gel chemistry have introduced new methods for controlling the rheological properties of coating solutions to accommodate BTESE colloids on substrates with larger pores. A pHswing method was proposed to effectively increase the colloidal parti­cle size of BTESE sol. By adjusting the pH in the post-hydrolyzed solu­tion, colloid particle size was increased to ~60 nm [21]. The larger particles were coated on substrates with 100 nm pore size forming an intermediate layer with pore size of 5 nm. The subsequent coating of BTESE layer showed enhanced gas permeance compared to those sup­ported on SiO2–ZrO2 layer [22]. However, the Si-OH groups are consumed under basic condition and shortens the shelf life of coating solution.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: hose sup­ported on SiO2–ZrO2 layer [22]. However, the Si-OH groups are consumed under basic condition and shortens the shelf life of coating solution. Moreover, the high sensitivity of pH-swing method to both temperature and reaction time poses significant challenges for scaling up. Due to the above-mentioned challenges, fabricating BTESE-derived silica membranes on substrates with larger pore sizes (e.g., with pore sizes >10 nm) remains a significant knowledge gap. The larger pore sizes allow for deeper infiltration of the coating solution which not only hampers gas sieving performance by creating non-selective paths but also undesirably increases the thickness of the membrane. Additionally, achieving a uniform coating over these larger pores often requires multiple layers, increasing the likelihood of defects and making the fabrication process more complex and less cost-effective. Therefore, optimizing the interaction between BTESE sol and substrates with different pore sizes is crucial for expanding the applicability of BTESE sol on a wider range of substrates. This study aims to address the challenges associated with fabricating hydrogen-selective BTESE membranes on substrates with a wide range of pore sizes. We focused on the effects of sol synthesis conditions and its performance when coated on mesoporous substrates, as well as the in­fluence of the drying conditions.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: the effects of sol synthesis conditions and its performance when coated on mesoporous substrates, as well as the in­fluence of the drying conditions. A reversible physical gelation of BTESE solution was observed with addition of organic salt as a surfactant to adjust the interparticle force. In addition, the viscosity of the BTESE solution was increased by adjusting the shear force and temperature during spin coating. By adjustment of interparticle force, we observed transition from shear thinning to shear thickening of the coating solu­tion. Applying the physically gelled coating solution leads to the for­mation of selective layers on mesoporous substrates (20–50 nm) with minimal pore filling. The performance of the resulting BTESE mem­branes is evaluated through gas permeation tests. This study is expected to provide deep insights into the gelation process and the fabrication details for the production of organosilica membranes, with excellent hydrogen sieving properties. 2. Experimental 2.1. Materials Porous γ-Al2O3 substrates (mean pore size 2, 5, 10, 20, 50 nm, thickness 2.0 mm, and diameter 25 mm in a disc geometry) were pur­chased from Foshan Yirun Fine ceramics. Bis(triethoxysilyl)ethane (BTESE, 96 % purity), anhydrous ethanol (EtOH) (99.9 %, Sigma Aldrich), nitric acid (HNO3) (concentration 70 %), and poly(ethylene glycol) with molecular weights of 400 Da, 1.5 kDa, 6 kDa, 25 kDa, 80 kDa, 500 kDa, and 5 MDa, were all sourced from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co., USA.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: e glycol) with molecular weights of 400 Da, 1.5 kDa, 6 kDa, 25 kDa, 80 kDa, 500 kDa, and 5 MDa, were all sourced from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co., USA. Milli-Q water with resistance of >15 MΩ-cm was obtained using a Merck Elix system. Deuterium oxide (D2O, 99.9 %) was purchased from Cambridge Isotope Laboratories, Inc.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: 2.2. Preparation of BTESE solution and derived membranes BTESE-derived organosilica sols were synthesized through hydroly­sis and polymerization reactions using water and HNO3 in anhydrous EtOH. The molar composition of the reactants was set to a BTESE/EtOH/ HNO3/H2O ratio of 1:X:0.01:100. The EtOH amount (X) was adjusted to control the concentration of BTESE. A sol was prepared by gradually adding BTESE dropwise into a HNO3–H2O–solvent mixture, maintained in an ice bath and stirred to control the hydrolysis process. Then, the mixture was heated in a refluxed oil bath at 60 ◦C for 1.5 h with continuous stirring to obtain the final hybrid sols. Subsequently, the mixture was cooled in an ice-bath to stop the reaction and allowed to stand in fridge prior to membrane fabrication and characterization. The alumina substrate was calcined at 1000 ◦C for 1 h prior to its use as a substrate. Subsequently, organosilica sol was spin-coated onto the substrate. For low temperature coating, the surface temperature of substrate was maintained by dry ice bath in coater chamber and measured to be 0 ± 1 ◦C with an infrared camera. The coated substrates were dried at 120 ◦C for 1 h, followed by calcination at 300 ◦C for 1 h at a ramp rate of 0.5 ◦C/min in air. The detailed spin coating process is described in Supplementary Information (SI text 1, Fig. S4) 2.3.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: ◦C for 1 h at a ramp rate of 0.5 ◦C/min in air. The detailed spin coating process is described in Supplementary Information (SI text 1, Fig. S4) 2.3. Characterization of BTESE sol and derived membranes The particle sizes and zeta potential of the freshly prepared hybrid sols were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Zeta-sizer Nano S, Malvern Instruments, UK) at 25 ◦C. The hydrodynamic diameter of the sol particles was determined from the Brownian motion of the particles, which is characterized by the translational diffusion coefficient (D) in the solvent. The hydrodynamic diameter (d) was calculated using the Stokes–Einstein equation: d = kT/3πηD, where k is the Boltzmann’s constant, T is the operational temperature, η is solvent viscosity. The rheological properties of the BTESE solutions were measured using a LAMY DSR500 rheometer at various temperatures controlled by a temperature-regulated cooling bath. The preparation of the cooling bath is as described in Ref. [23] Before testing, the specimens were conditioned at the desired temperature for 15 min to remove any ther­mal and shear history. Frequency sweep tests were performed in oscil­latory mode over a range of 0.05–500 rad/s. The strain level was determined via a strain sweep test to ensure that all measurements were within the linear viscoelastic regime. The surface roughness of substrate was characterized by mapping conducted on a 3D laser scanning microscope. (LEXT OLS4100, Olympus, Japan).

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: c regime. The surface roughness of substrate was characterized by mapping conducted on a 3D laser scanning microscope. (LEXT OLS4100, Olympus, Japan). The surface morphology and thickness of the mem­brane were determined by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; model: Zeiss Merlin, Germany). An acceleration voltage of 5 kV was used for all samples. The working distance was kept at 5–10 mm depending on sample size. Iridium coating was sputtered onto the sample surface with an estimated thickness of 3 nm prior to SEM imaging. 2.4. Substrate pore size measurement Substrate pore sizes were determined by molecular weight cut off (MWCO) measurements according to a method recommended by See Toh et al. [24] PEG with molecular weights ranging from 400 Da to 5 MDa was used as a probe due to its spherical shape. For each test, 100 mg of each standard was dissolved in 10 g of D2O at 90 ◦C. The solutions were prepared in the same way except for the 5 MDa PEG standard so­lution for which only 50 mg of PEG-5 million was dissolved in 10g of D2O due to its solubility limitations. MWCO measurements were con­ducted using 10 g of D2O feed solution, continuously agitated at 500 rpm to minimize concentration polarization. Rejection characterization of the fresh membranes was carried out in a vacuum-assisted filtration system. Rejection of the membranes was measured under the same standard process conditions, with a trans­

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: X. Jin et al. Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123826 membrane pressure of 1 * 106 Pa, at 20 ◦C. The MWCO was determined by interpolating from the plot of rejection against molecular weight of marker compounds and corresponds to the molecular weight at which rejection reaches 90 %. Rejection of markers was calculated from equation (2) where the CP,i and CF,i correspond to marker concentrations in the permeate and the feed, respectively. Ri =(1 -CP,i CF,i )* 100%(2) Concentrations were obtained from NMR analysis calibrated by an external standard method. For analysis the samples were injected into 5 mm O.D. NMR tubes and sealed. The sample height was restricted to approximately 50 mm to ensure that all of the sample was located within the transmitter/receiver coil and thus was homogenously irradiated. Effective pore size was estimated with Stokes radius of PEG at 90 % rejection, as shown in equation (3) [25]: ds = 16.73 * 10-12 * M0.557(3)

content: 2.5. Permeation test of the BTESE-derived silica membranes Single-gas permeation tests for H2 and N2 were conducted to eval­uate the performance of planar BTESE-derived silica membranes using the constant-volume/variable-pressure method in a custom-built rig. Samples were partially masked along the rim using impermeable aluminium tape on the upstream face, or on both the upstream and downstream faces, as described elsewhere [26]. The aluminium tape used was Reinforced Aluminium Foil Tape (supplied by Precision Paper Coatings, Australia), which is impermeable and resistant to the experi­mental conditions. The membrane area available for gas permeation after masking was ~1.7 cm2. The masked membranes were placed in a stainless-steel cell and sealed with Viton 75 O-rings to prevent leakage. Both the upstream and downstream sides of the system were degassed for at least 1 h, ensuring an outgassing rate of less than 1 %. The degassing process was conducted at room temperature under vacuum for a prolonged period till outgassing rate from the system and sample was below measurement limit of our pressure transducer. Gas per­meance measurements were then performed at room temperature which was monitored by a temperature sensor installed close to the permeation tank. Pure gases (H2, and N2) were fed into the membrane individually, and the permeance of each pure gas was measured at the feed pressure of 2 bar. The gas permeance (P) was calculated using Eq.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: e membrane individually, and the permeance of each pure gas was measured at the feed pressure of 2 bar. The gas permeance (P) was calculated using Eq. (1) [26]: Pi =(dpdt )VAΔpRT(1) Here, dp/dt is the slope (Pa/s) of the pressure against time in the pseudo-steady state; V is the downstream volume (cm3), which is either 50.43 cm3 or 14066 cm3 in our case, selection of which is based on the expected permeance of the membrane. In equation (1), the Δp is the difference in the pressure between the two sides of the membrane in Pascal (Pa) at time zero; A is the effective membrane area (cm2); R is gas constant of 37.06 Pa cm3/(cm3(STP)K); T is membrane temperature in Kelvin. The ideal selectivity coefficient of membrane for gas H2 gas with respect to N2, that is, H2/N2 selectivity is defined as the ratio of the permeance of H2 and N2 (PH2 and PN2). 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Property of substrates The SEM images reveal the morphological differences and pore size variations among the substrates (Fig. 1A–E). For substrates with pore sizes of 2 and 5 nm, a dense surface is observed, indicating the absence of interconnecting channels. In contrast, substrates with pore sizes greater than 10 nm display discernible edges of alumina particles. Larger pore sizes, such as 20 and 50 nm, are associated with loosely packed particles, distinguishing them from the compact nanoporous structures observed in smaller pore size substrates.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: nm, are associated with loosely packed particles, distinguishing them from the compact nanoporous structures observed in smaller pore size substrates. Table 1 summarizes the physical prop­erties of all substrates. The 90 % MWCO method (Fig. 1F) of substrate 1–5 corresponds to calculated pore size from 1.6 nm to 56.1 nm. This

content: X. Jin et al. Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123826 result agrees with the nominal pore size provided by the supplier (Table 2). The nitrogen permeance test (at 1 bar of transmembrane pressure and room temperature) was also used to characterize the pore size of all substrates. With increased pore size, the nitrogen permeance increases linearly. Substrates obtained from different batches showed a pore size error margin of 2–3% in pore size, indicating good reproduc­ibility of substrates. Surface roughness data obtained using laser mi­croscopy revealed a positive correlation between surface roughness and substrate pore size in the range of 0.001–0.034 μm. This correlation is expected [27], as the substrates were prepared by calcinating γ-alumina powder with different particle sizes. Higher roughness is also anticipated to result in greater pore-filling effects during coating, which will be discussed in the following section. 3.2. Effect of substrate pore size The influence of substrate pore size ranging from 2 nm to 50 nm on the formation of BTESE derived silica membranes was investigated. A concentration of 5 % was initially selected, as it is a widely used con­dition for both dip and spin coating [28–30]. Coatings on substrate 1 with a pore size of 2 nm were expected to be effective. From Fig. 2A, the first coating resulted in mild infiltration, as demonstrated by a 13.7 % loss in H₂ permeance while maintaining a selectivity above Knudsen value (3.7).

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: rst coating resulted in mild infiltration, as demonstrated by a 13.7 % loss in H₂ permeance while maintaining a selectivity above Knudsen value (3.7). An interconnected hydrogen-selective layer began forming after the second coating. Following four coating cycles, the selectivity peaked at ~20, which is comparable to reported values for membranes prepared using precursor solutions with similar hydrolysis ratios [13]. Hydrogen permeance decreased with additional coating cycles due to increased membrane thickness. Coating beyond four cycles did not improve H2/N2 selectivity but only decreased gas permeance, suggesting the formation of a defect-free, continuous layer. For substrate 2 with pore size of 5 nm (Fig. 2B), the first coating led to a significant decrease in H₂ permeance and a corresponding increase in selectivity. A pronounced pore-filling effect was observed during the initial coatings, likely due to the small colloidal particle size (1–2 nm) relative to the larger pore size (~4–5 nm). Once the surface pores were filled, subsequent coatings formed a sufficiently continuous surface layer, achieving a selectivity ~20 after the fifth coating. Notably, SiO₂ZrO₂ substrates with similar pore sizes have also been widely studied as intermediate layers, typically requiring 2–4 repetitive coatings [31–33]. This underscores the ubiquitous nature of the pore-filling effect in sol-gel synthesis of inorganic membranes.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: uiring 2–4 repetitive coatings [31–33]. This underscores the ubiquitous nature of the pore-filling effect in sol-gel synthesis of inorganic membranes. For substrates with larger pore sizes (10, 20 and 50 nm), different outcomes were observed. For substrate 3 with pore size of 10 nm (Fig. 2C), H₂ permeance decreased after the first and second coatings, but without a corresponding improvement in selectivity. This suggests that larger pores allowed excessive infiltration of the coating solution, hindering the formation of a stable and selective membrane layer. After three coatings, a mild increase in selectivity and a reduction in H₂ per­meance were observed, indicating the formation of a silica network within the substrate pores. However, pinhole defects persisted, with selectivity only reaching 14.4 after five coatings. The trend was more pronounced for substrate 4 (20 nm, Fig. 2D), where a significant drop in H₂ permeance occurred, but no substantial improvement in selectivity was observed until the fourth coating. Attempts to coat substrate 5 with pore size of 50 nm (Fig. 2E) were unsuccessful. The large pore sizes caused excessive infiltration, pre­venting the formation of a continuous membrane layer on the tope of substrate, even after multiple coatings. Additionally, the high surface roughness of substrate 5 further contributed to the loss of coating so­lution during the spin coating process.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: oatings. Additionally, the high surface roughness of substrate 5 further contributed to the loss of coating so­lution during the spin coating process. Substrates with such large pore sizes were thus deemed ineffective for selective membrane formation. Effective coating of BTESE colloids onto substrates with smaller pore size, such as substrate 1 (2 nm) and 2 (5 nm), is expected due to their dense surfaces, which mitigated infiltration and allowed the formation of thin membranes. This is consistent with the trends observed in Fig. 2F, where these substrates exhibit both high H₂ permeance and H₂/N₂ selectivity, indicating uniform and defect-free membrane layers. Inter­estingly, after 3–4 coating cycles, a H₂-selective membrane layer was also formed on substrates 3 and 4 with larger pore sizes. However, a sharp decline in selectivity with increasing pore size was found in Fig. 2F, suggesting that larger-pore substrates may allow severe infil­tration of colloids, leading to less selective membrane. Coating BTESE solution directly onto substrates with larger pore sizes >20 nm is desirable, as it could simplify the fabrication process and potentially lower the fabrication costs. It is hypothesized that mem­branes with poor selectivity formed on substrates with larger pore size were likely due to the condensation of colloidal particles during spin coating, which promoted solvent evaporation and triggered network formation within the substrate pores.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: ondensation of colloidal particles during spin coating, which promoted solvent evaporation and triggered network formation within the substrate pores. To test this hypothesis, we modi­fied the parameters of the coating solution for substrate 4 (nominal pore size of 20 nm) with aim to achieve a continuous coating characterized by high selectivity and reduced pore-filling effects. 3.3. Effect of coating conditions 3.3.1. Effect of BTESE concentration The effect of BTESE concentration was studied using substrate 4 (20 nm). As shown in Table 1, the Si:H₂O ratio was maintained at 100, and the acid catalyst ratio was kept at 0.01. Ethanol content was varied to adjust the BTESE concentration. Higher concentrations of BTESE were expected to produce larger colloidal particles due to an increased hy­drolysis reaction rate, potentially reducing the pore-filling effect on the mesoporous substrate. Dynamic light scattering confirmed this hy­pothesis, showing particle sizes of 1.3, 1.5, and 4.2 nm for 5 wt%, 10 wt %, and 15 wt% solutions, respectively (Fig. 3A). Apart from different particle size, the viscosities of the BTESE increases with initial concen­tration (Fig. 3B). The pore-filling effect was evaluated by applying a single layer of each solution to substrate 4, followed by calcination. Gas permeance results are shown in Fig. 3C. The effect of coating solution concentration was evident even for a single layer.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: followed by calcination. Gas permeance results are shown in Fig. 3C. The effect of coating solution concentration was evident even for a single layer. Despite the marginal increase in solution viscosity, the 10 wt% coating solution exhibited the highest selectivity of 3.4 and resulted in an 83 % reduction in permeance compared to the uncoated substrate. In contrast, the 15 wt% BTESE solution resulted in a lower permeance loss of 78 % but exhibited a significantly reduced selectivity. This discrepancy is likely due to the increased colloidal particle size in the 15 wt% solution, which may lead to less uniform coating coverage and reduced packing density. Conse­quently, this results in larger residual pores and diminished selectivity in the membrane. The resulting pore size in the silica membrane depends on the packing density of the particles. Furthermore, high alkoxysilane concentrations are less favorable for acid-catalyzed cyclization re­actions, as they result in larger pores that are less selective for hydrogen [34]. Repeated coatings using a 10 wt% solution was conducted to eval­uate its pore-filling behaviour and ability to form a continuous, selective

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: Calculated Pore Size (nm) Nominal Pore Size (nm) Roughness (um) Substrate 1 2948 ± 551.61.620.001 Substrate 2 5051 ± 621153.450.004 Substrate 3 6469 ± 10549711.0100.010 Substrate 4 10,451 ±141 355621.1200.019 Substrate 5 21,202 ±219 668456.1500.034 X. Jin et al. Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123826 membrane layer (Fig. 3D). Compared to the 5 wt% solution, which required five coatings to achieve a selectivity of ~10, the 10 wt% so­lution demonstrated superior performance, reaching higher selectivity (~13) after the same number of coating cycles. This improvement can be attributed to the higher viscosity and concentration of the 10 wt% so­lution, which facilitated better control over the coating process and enhanced the formation of a uniform, continuous layer with fewer defects. In contrast, the 15 wt% solution, despite its higher viscosity and limited infiltration due to larger colloidal particle size, exhibited the lowest selectivity (~5.5) after five coatings. This result is attributed to insufficient polymerization of the BTESE colloids during the coating process. The –OH groups, essential for condensation reactions and network formation, were consumed during cyclization, preventing the formation of a dense, defect-free silica layer. Consequently, pinholes and structural defects compromised the molecular sieving properties, resulting in lower selectivity despite the reduced infiltration into the substrate pores [13,27,35].

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: cts compromised the molecular sieving properties, resulting in lower selectivity despite the reduced infiltration into the substrate pores [13,27,35]. Overall, the 10 wt% solution was the most effective condition for forming a hydrogen-selective membrane on substrate 4. However, it is worth noting that the resulting membrane had an H₂ permeance of ~290

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: GPU, which is significantly lower than the state-of-the-art performance [11,36]. This low permeance is attributed to the thick effective layer, originating from infiltration, with an effective thickness of nearly 2 μm. (The as-prepared hybrid silica synthesized under our conditions exhibited an H₂ permeability of ~180 Barrer.) 3.3.2. Effect of solution aging Aging of the coating solution by storing it at 40–60 ◦C allows for slow polymerization of the sol to increase viscosity [33,36]. The pH adjust­ment (pH swing) has a similar effect and has been shown to facilitate the formation of a continuous layer on substrates with larger pore sizes (up to 100 nm) [21]. Effect of solution ageing was studied using the sub­strate 4 with pore size of 20 nm and results are shown in Fig. 4. The gelation time for alkoxysilanes depends on factors such as water ratio, acid ratio, temperature, and initial concentration [20], with additional influence from the bridging units in organically bridged alkoxysilanes [18]. The 10 wt% BTESE solution was selected for the aging study due to its controllable pore-filling properties, as discussed in the previous section. Aging was conducted at 40 ◦C in an oven and at room temperature (RT) in a closed, dark cabinet. Four weeks of aging at 40 ◦C has been found effective in modifying the rheological properties of

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

Fig. 4. Effect of aged coating solution on substrate 4. (A) Particle size and (B) viscosity of aged solutions prepared with an initial BTESE concentration of 10 wt%. (C) Pore-filling effect of fresh and aged solutions on substrate 4 after the first coating. citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: BTESE colloids for improved dip coating [33] and these conditions have been used in this study. Fresh solutions and BTESE solutions aged at RT for the same period served as control groups. Aging at 40 ◦C promoted the chemical gelation of the BTESE solu­tion, as indicated by a slight increase in viscosity (Fig. 4A). The colloid particle size showed minimal increase after four weeks of aging (Fig. 4B). This outcome was expected because dimerization, the primary driver of colloid particle growth, had already approached equilibrium during sol synthesis, thereby minimizing precipitation [35]. As shown in Fig. 4C, aging had a marginal effect on pore filling, resulting in approximately 80 % hydrogen permeation loss after the first coating compared to the uncoated substrate. Repetitive coating with the aged solution at 40 ◦C resulted in a membrane with a lower H₂ permeance of 413 ± 37 GPU and an selectivity of 13.8. However, this selectivity was only slightly higher than that of either control group (Fig. 4D). The effect of solution aging appears marginal when coating porous substrates. Previous studies have demonstrated that aging is beneficial for BTESE membrane synthesis on substrates. With pore size of 2–5 nm [33,36]. The results presented above highlight the effectiveness of vis­cosity control for microporous substrates.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: ubstrates. With pore size of 2–5 nm [33,36]. The results presented above highlight the effectiveness of vis­cosity control for microporous substrates. However, it is worth noting that the aging of the coating solution for such a long period poses challenges in batch control and increases additional investment in time. 3.3.3. Effect of drying conditions In the sol-gel method, the drying step is essential for solvent evap­oration and triggering polymerization of alkoxysilane colloids. This step also influences pore filling, as the solvent evaporation rate affects the local viscosity of the wet-coated layer. For nanoporous substrates, a typical drying protocol involves room temperature drying, followed by heating at 120 ◦C for 0.5–1 h to ensure solvent removal [13]. For microporous substrates, prolonged drying time at room temperature might cause excessive infiltration. To assess the drying effects, a 10 wt% BTESE solution was applied to substrate 4. The first group of samples underwent normal drying at room temperature for 1 h, followed by oven drying at 120 ◦C for 0.5 h before calcination at 300 ◦C for 0.5 h. These were labelled as “Normal.” Another group skipped room-temperature drying and underwent direct oven drying and calcination, labelled as “Reduced.” As shown in Fig. 5A, the normal drying resulted in greater infiltra­tion, as indicated by lower permeance, accompanied by slightly higher H₂/N₂ selectivity.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: wn in Fig. 5A, the normal drying resulted in greater infiltra­tion, as indicated by lower permeance, accompanied by slightly higher H₂/N₂ selectivity. The reduced drying process showed improved per­meance but did not significantly impact selectivity. Both methods yiel­ded membranes with permeance below 1000 GPU, which is considered suboptimal. An alternative approach involved drying the coated samples in an environmental chamber with controlled relative humidity. Chambers were custom-built, with humidity maintained using saturated salt so­lutions [37]. Samples stored under higher relative humidity exhibited slightly improved selectivity due to slower solvent evaporation, which promotes further cyclization of siloxanes, leading to a denser silica network (Fig. 5B) [13,34]. 3.3.4. Effect of physical gelation In the sol-gel method, viscosity naturally increases due to polymer­ization, which reduces the pore-filling effect. As the solvent evaporates, the coating solution’s concentration increases, promoting dimerization and the formation of a non-reversible network [21]. The attempts dis­cussed above indicate that chemical gelation can increase the viscosity of the coating solution but is not effective due to low controllability. Increasing the concentration promotes the growth of colloidal particle size, reducing the permselectivity of the resulting membrane. Both aging and drying have a marginal effect on reducing infiltration into micro­porous substrate.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: cing the permselectivity of the resulting membrane. Both aging and drying have a marginal effect on reducing infiltration into micro­porous substrate. Physical gelation offers a more dynamic approach by manipulating the external and internal forces affecting colloidal particles, such as temperature, shear force, and interparticle forces [38]. During physical gelation, materials transition into a solid-like state where movement of particles becomes limited due to changes in these external forces. Physical gelation occurs when these forces limit particle movement, transitioning the material into a solid-like state (Fig. S2). A common method to enhancing physical gelation in colloids is the addition of salt to adjust isoelectric point [38]. Due to the wide application of colloidal solution, physical gelation has become an area of growing interest [39,40]. The hypothesis here is that by controlling temperature, shear force and interparticle force in coating solution, we can adjust the rheological behaviour of the coating solution and reduce the pore-filling effect on microporous substrates. Trials with inorganic salts (NaCl, KCl) and organic salts (SDS, sodium dodecyl sulfate, and SLES, sodium laureth sulfate) were conducted by mixing them with a 10 wt% BTESE solution. Mixing was performed both before and after hydrolysis to evaluate compatibility. Viscosity tests at room temperature screened effective components.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: n. Mixing was performed both before and after hydrolysis to evaluate compatibility. Viscosity tests at room temperature screened effective components. Table 3 summarizes these results, with diagonal cells indicating coagulated solutions. Chlo­ride salts showed negligible effects on viscosity, while lowconcentration organic salts effectively altered the rheological behavior without causing precipitation. The viscosity outcome measured at room temperature are summarized in Table 3. The diagonal cells represent coagulated solutions. Both chloride salts were found to be ineffective in changing the viscosity of colloidal solution, regardless of their concen­tration. Mixing inorganic salts at a concentration of 15 mM before hy­drolysis led to coagulation. Unlike inorganic salts, organic salts, being surfactant, can form colloids independently with a critical micelle con­centration (CMC) of SDS and SLES were 7.16 mM and 5.01 mM, respectively [41,42]. To avoid formation of an organic salt colloid (micelle), concentrations significantly lower than their CMCs were used. However, due to the shift in isoelectric point, even the lowest concen­tration of organic salt caused precipitation of BTESE solution during hydrolysis. Therefore, only post-hydrolysis mixing of organic salts was examined. As shown in Table 3, SDS at 0.5 mM was the most effective in

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: Fig. 5. Effect of drying conditions. (A) Effect of normal and reduced drying between coating cycles. Four coatings were applied on substrate 4. (B) Effect of drying with controlled relative humidity in home-made environmental chamber at room temperature. X. Jin et al. Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123826 increasing viscosity of the coating solution. We continued with a more comprehensive rheological analysis of BTESE solution mixed with SDS. The rheological analysis (Fig. 6) demonstrates the impact of viscosity on the performance of SDS-BTESE derived membranes. The particle size distribution (Fig. 6A) shows that the addition of SDS does not signifi­cantly increase particle size, which is crucial for maintaining the con­sistency of the coating solution and preventing non-selective voids after calcination. Zeta potential value of SDS-BTESE shows slightly reduction compared to BTESE colloid, due to the adsorption of surfacntat on BTESE colloids (Fig. S3). The relatively high zeta potential (17.8 mV) of SDS-BTESE at low pH indicates the stability of the solution. In Fig. 6B, the temperature-dependent viscosity indicates that as the temperature decreases, the viscosity of both BTESE and SDS-BTESE so­lutions increases. This trend is widely observed in colloid systems. This rise in viscosity, particularly in SDS-BTESE, is likely due to the arrested Brownian motion of colloidal particles.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: observed in colloid systems. This rise in viscosity, particularly in SDS-BTESE, is likely due to the arrested Brownian motion of colloidal particles. This effect may reduce deep infiltration into larger substrate pores and promotes better surface coverage of the coating solution. Fig. 6C reveals the time-dependent viscosity behaviour, showing an exponential decay of viscosity after vigorous stirring. This suggests a relaxation process driven by physical gelation, where the balance of repulsive and attractive forces stabilizes the coating solution. When the external shear force is removed, the system fluidizes again. Fig. 6D further highlights the shear rate-dependent viscosity of BTESE and SDSBTESE. The BTESE solution shows slightly shear-thinning behaviour, which agrees with previous literature [43]. However, SDS-BTESE ex­hibits shear-thickening behaviour at -21.3 ◦C. Without organic salt additive, the BTESE colloid particle are mildly attractive to each other due to the hydrogen bonding stemming from the abundant S–OH group on their surface. These attractive interparticle forces enable colloid particles to quickly align with the flow direction, resulting in macro­scopic shear-thinning behaviour. With the addition of organic salt, the colloid particles become slightly repulsive to each other due to the electrostatic force. The repulsive force created a network of frictional contacts between particles [39].

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: slightly repulsive to each other due to the electrostatic force. The repulsive force created a network of frictional contacts between particles [39]. Upon shearing, the additional friction network between colloid particles restricts particle movement, resulting in macroscopic shear-thickening behaviour. These characteristics indi­cate that under shearing and cooling conditions, the SDS-BTESE solution showed reversible physical gelation behaviour.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: 3.3.5. SDS-BTESE performance Taking advantage of the physical gelation of BTESE solution, we synthesized silica membrane under shear and temperature control using spin coating. Unlike conventional BTESE solution, the physically gelled BTESE solution was able to form continuous membrane on substrate with larger pore size, as shown in Fig. 7. Fig. 7A shows the crosssectional SEM image of SDS-BTESE membrane supported on an alumina substrate. The image was obtained by focused ion beam (FIB), with a protective carbon coating (~1 μm thick) deposited on the surface of membrane beforehand. The effective coating thickness is about ~0.86 μm, with minimal infiltration into substrate. The permselectivity of the membrane on the 20 nm pore substrate with varying coating cycles is shown in Fig. 7B. The increased viscosity achieved through physical gelation results in a mild reduction in H₂ permeance and a significant increase in selectivity as the number of coating cycles in­crease. The controlled viscosity effectively prevents excessive infiltra­tion, allowing the formation of a continuous, defect-free membrane layer. In contrast, without physical gelation, as shown in previous sec­tion Fig. 2D, the standard BTESE solution could only form a selective membrane on smaller pores, such as those with 5 nm. We further tested the coating solution onto substrate with even larger pore sizes.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: orm a selective membrane on smaller pores, such as those with 5 nm. We further tested the coating solution onto substrate with even larger pore sizes. On substrate 5, a substantial decrease in H₂ permeance was observed, but the improvement in selectivity remained modest. For the substrates with pore size of 100 nm, excessive infiltration persisted, limiting the membrane’s ability to form a stable and selective layer. Although physical gelation and viscosity adjustments help mitigate this issue, achieving a fully selective membrane on substrates with 50 and 100 nm pores still remains a significant challenge. Plotting the coating cycle against resistance (the reciprocal of H₂ permeance, Fig. S5) reveals that the first three coatings on the 100 nm substrate did not contribute to membrane formation, as indicated by a rapid increase in resistance with minimal selectivity improvement. In contrast, for the 20 nm and 50 nm substrates, gas resistance and H₂/N₂ selectivity increased simultaneously with additional coating cycles, confirming successful membrane formation with the SDS-BTESE layer. By comparing the results in Figs. 6 and 7, it is evident that physical gelation and viscosity control are crucial for expanding the pore size range suitable for selective membrane formation.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: 7, it is evident that physical gelation and viscosity control are crucial for expanding the pore size range suitable for selective membrane formation. The shear-thickening behaviours of SDS-BTESE, combined with the increased viscosity at reduced temperatures, allows for better surface coverage on larger pore substrates, making the formation of hydrogen-selective membranes possible across a wider range of pore sizes. It is worth noting that the temperature control of the substrate is critical due to the sensitivity of physical gelation to both shear rate and temperature. Relaxing the gelled solution by removing shear force results in the solution liquefying within 5 min (Fig. 6C). Moreover, the SDS-BTESE colloid exhibits similar vis­cosity to the BTESE colloid at room temperature. No improvement in gas separation performance is observed when coating is conducted at room temperature. This is expected, as SDS is insufficient to prevent BTESE colloid infiltration at this temperature. Thus, reduced temperature (below 0 ◦C), high shear rate (>2000 rpm), and surfactant regulation are all contribute to physical gelation and ensure effective coverage of substrates with pore sizes larger than 20 nm. Further exploration of more scalable methods is needed to overcome the challenge of precisely controlling substrate temperature during coating. Fig.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: . Further exploration of more scalable methods is needed to overcome the challenge of precisely controlling substrate temperature during coating. Fig. 8 presents a trade-off plot for H₂/N₂ separation, showcasing microporous inorganic membranes such as silica, carbon molecular sieves, and high decomposition temperature metal-organic frameworks (HT MOFs). Zeolite membranes, which are not commonly used for this gas pair, are excluded from the figure. More details including membrane synthesis method, testing condition, stability and membrane configu­ration are listed in Table S1. Compared to the membranes highlighted, the SDS-BTESE-derived silica membrane demonstrates comparable separation performance to the state-of-the-art. Notably, it is the only membrane directly coated on a mesoporous substrate, offering signifi­cant potential for cost-effective gas separation membrane applications.

citation.title: Effect of substrate pore size and physical gelation on the performance of BTESE-derived silica membranes for gas separation

content: 4. Conclusion This work demonstrates the importance of substrate pore size and rheological control in forming hydrogen-selective BTESE-derived silica membranes. High performance hydrogen selective membrane has been successfully synthesized using a spin-coating method on alumina sub­strate with smaller pore size (2–5 nm). Adjusting synthesis conditions, such as initial BTESE concentration, interparticle forces, temperature, and physical gelation, enable the formation of selective membranes on larger substrates with pore sizes up to 20 nm. The rheological analysis reveals that increased viscosity, driven by shear-thickening behaviour under physical gelation, prevents deep infiltration into larger pores, leading to continuous, defect-free layer formation. This development addresses the limitations of conventional BTESE membranes, which were previously restricted to smaller pore sizes. The ability to control viscosity through physical gelation expands the application range for hydrogen separation membranes. These find­ings offer valuable insights for optimizing membrane fabrication, particularly for larger pore substrates, and demonstrate the potential of BTESE derived silica membranes in industrial gas separation applications. Derrick Ng: Writing – review & editing, Methodology.
